title: Working with Backends
text: call left_join_spy() join_strict() without specifying backend, joinspy inspects class x y picks backend according fixed priority: data.table > tibble > base R. data.table takes priority merge implementation depends key handling, indexing, reference semantics dplyr join discard. dplyr, hand, handles coerced data.table without issues. inputs checked – one side tibble plain data frame, dplyr selected. mixed-class call selects backend whose package installed, joinspy falls back base R warning. detection action input type: two inputs different classes, higher-priority class wins:
code: # Base R data frames: auto-detects "base" orders_df <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers_df <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result_base <- left_join_spy(orders_df, customers_df, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_base) #> [1] "data.frame" # Tibbles: auto-detects "dplyr" orders_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  customers_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana") )  result_dplyr <- left_join_spy(orders_tbl, customers_tbl, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_dplyr) #> [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame" # data.tables: auto-detects "data.table" orders_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  customers_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana") )  result_dt <- left_join_spy(orders_dt, customers_dt, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result_dt) #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame" # data.table + tibble: data.table wins mixed_result <- left_join_spy(orders_dt, customers_tbl, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(mixed_result) #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame"

title: Working with Backends
text: join wrappers join_strict() accept backend argument overrides auto-detection. three valid values "base", "dplyr", "data.table". can force dplyr plain data frames get tibble output: force base R sidestep dplyr’s many--many warning already know expansion intentional: force data.table plain data frames speed large inputs: explicit backend must installed. Requesting backend = "dplyr" without dplyr error, silently fall back – auto-detection convenience, explicit override contract. Setting backend = "base" also way guarantee reproducibility across environments dplyr may may installed.
code: result <- left_join_spy(orders_df, customers_df, by = "id",                         backend = "dplyr", .quiet = TRUE) class(result) #> [1] "data.frame" # These have a legitimate many-to-many relationship tags <- dplyr::tibble(   item_id = c(1, 1, 2),   tag = c("red", "large", "small") )  prices <- dplyr::tibble(   item_id = c(1, 2, 2),   currency = c("USD", "USD", "EUR") )  # Force base R to avoid dplyr's many-to-many warning result <- left_join_spy(tags, prices, by = "item_id",                         backend = "base", .quiet = TRUE) nrow(result) #> [1] 4 result <- left_join_spy(orders_df, customers_df, by = "id",                         backend = "data.table", .quiet = TRUE) class(result) #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame"

title: Working with Backends
text: joinspy preserves input class full diagnostic-repair-join cycle: Diagnostics (join_spy(), key_check(), etc.) accept data frame subclass return report objects without modifying input. Repair (join_repair()) operates key columns place returns class received. Join wrappers dispatch native join engine detected class. full cycle base R data frames: cycle tibbles: data.tables: join_repair() receives x y, returns list $x $y, preserving class corresponding input. join_strict() also preserves class – cardinality check runs join, satisfied constraint returns native class violated one errors output produced. one exception explicit backend override match input class. Passing backend = "data.table" tibble returns data.table, data.table engine produces.
code: messy_df <- data.frame(   code = c("A-1 ", "B-2", " C-3"),   value = c(10, 20, 30),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  lookup_df <- data.frame(   code = c("A-1", "B-2", "C-3"),   label = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # 1. Diagnose report <- join_spy(messy_df, lookup_df, by = "code")  # 2. Repair repaired_df <- join_repair(messy_df, by = "code") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) class(repaired_df)  # still data.frame #> [1] "data.frame"  # 3. Join joined_df <- left_join_spy(repaired_df, lookup_df, by = "code", .quiet = TRUE) class(joined_df)  # still data.frame #> [1] "data.frame" joined_df #>   code value label #> 1  A-1    10 Alpha #> 2  B-2    20  Beta #> 3  C-3    30 Gamma messy_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   code = c("A-1 ", "B-2", " C-3"),   value = c(10, 20, 30) )  lookup_tbl <- dplyr::tibble(   code = c("A-1", "B-2", "C-3"),   label = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma") )  repaired_tbl <- join_repair(messy_tbl, by = "code") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) class(repaired_tbl)  # still tbl_df #> [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame"  joined_tbl <- left_join_spy(repaired_tbl, lookup_tbl, by = "code", .quiet = TRUE) class(joined_tbl)  # still tbl_df #> [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame" joined_tbl #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   code  value label #>   <chr> <dbl> <chr> #> 1 A-1      10 Alpha #> 2 B-2      20 Beta  #> 3 C-3      30 Gamma messy_dt <- data.table::data.table(   code = c("A-1 ", "B-2", " C-3"),   value = c(10, 20, 30) )  lookup_dt <- data.table::data.table(   code = c("A-1", "B-2", "C-3"),   label = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma") )  repaired_dt <- join_repair(messy_dt, by = "code") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) class(repaired_dt)  # still data.table #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame"  joined_dt <- left_join_spy(repaired_dt, lookup_dt, by = "code", .quiet = TRUE) class(joined_dt)  # still data.table #> [1] "data.table" "data.frame" joined_dt #> Key: <code> #>      code value  label #>    <char> <num> <char> #> 1:    A-1    10  Alpha #> 2:    B-2    20   Beta #> 3:    C-3    30  Gamma

title: Working with Backends
text: diagnostic functions (join_spy(), key_check(), key_duplicates(), join_explain(), detect_cardinality(), check_cartesian()) operate purely column values never call join engine. produce identical results regardless input class. means can diagnose data.tables join dplyr, diagnose base-R script pass data Shiny app uses dplyr internally. report object structurally identical across backends – $issues, $expected_rows, $match_analysis contain values. also means can write unit tests key quality using plain data frames even production code uses data.table.
code: # Diagnose on data.tables orders_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  customers_dt <- data.table::data.table(   id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Diana") )  report <- join_spy(orders_dt, customers_dt, by = "id")  # Join with dplyr (convert first) orders_tbl <- dplyr::as_tibble(orders_dt) customers_tbl <- dplyr::as_tibble(customers_dt) result <- left_join_spy(orders_tbl, customers_tbl, by = "id", .quiet = TRUE) class(result) #> [1] "tbl_df"     "tbl"        "data.frame"

title: Working with Backends
text: three backends differ ways worth noting: Column name collisions. Base R dplyr append .x/.y suffixes; data.table appends . right-table columns. Row ordering. Base R sorts key; dplyr preserves left-table order; data.table sorts key keyed, otherwise preserves insertion order. Performance. data.table fastest large inputs. Base R dplyr comparable small medium datasets. switch backends mid-project, worth checking column references row-order assumptions still hold.

title: Common Join Problems
text: classic. Someone exports CSV Excel, now half keys carry trailing space. Everything looks fine print data frame. Nothing matches join. Two three keys carry whitespace prevents matching. join_repair() strips : Passing dry_run = TRUE previews repair without applying . problem compounds composite keys – whitespace column enough break match: warehouse product carry trailing spaces shipments, three rows fail match. single join_repair() call cleans every key column :
code: sales <- data.frame(   product = c("Widget", "Gadget ", " Gizmo"),   units = c(10, 20, 30),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  inventory <- data.frame(   product = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stock = c(100, 200, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(sales, inventory, by = "product") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: product #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 1 #> Keys only in left: 2 #> Keys only in right: 2 #> Match rate (left): "33.3%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'product' has 2 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 2 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Gadget ' ~ 'Gadget', ' Gizmo' ~ 'Gizmo') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 1 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 5 sales_clean <- join_repair(sales, by = "product") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) key_check(sales_clean, inventory, by = "product") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected shipments <- data.frame(   warehouse = c("East ", "West", "East "),   product   = c("Widget", "Gadget ", "Gizmo"),   shipped   = c(50, 80, 35),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  stock <- data.frame(   warehouse = c("East", "West", "East"),   product   = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   on_hand   = c(200, 150, 90),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(shipments, stock, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: warehouse, product #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 0 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "0%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'warehouse' has 1 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 1 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'East ' ~ 'East') - possible typos? #> ! Left column 'product' has 1 value(s) with leading/trailing whitespace #> ℹ 1 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Gadget ' ~ 'Gadget') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Per-Column Breakdown ── #>  #> warehouse: "50%" match rate (1/2) #> product: "66.7%" match rate (2/3) #> ℹ Lowest match rate: warehouse #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 0 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 6 shipments_clean <- join_repair(shipments, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) key_check(shipments_clean, stock, by = c("warehouse", "product")) #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

title: Common Join Problems
text: Databases often case-insensitive; R . pull tables two different systems, one might store "ABC" "abc". None keys match -: can repair sides common case: "lower", "upper", "title" work. One thing watch : key column factor, case standardization changes level labels underlying integer codes. Converting character first avoids silently merging distinct factor levels.
code: sensors <- data.frame(   station = c("AWS-01", "aws-02", "Aws-03"),   temp = c(22.1, 18.4, 25.7),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  metadata <- data.frame(   station = c("aws-01", "AWS-02", "AWS-03"),   region = c("North", "South", "East"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) join_spy(sensors, metadata, by = "station") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: station #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 0 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "0%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! 3 key(s) would match if case-insensitive (e.g., 'AWS-01' vs 'aws-01') #> ℹ 5 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'AWS-01' ~ 'AWS-02', 'AWS-01' ~ 'AWS-03', 'aws-02' ~ 'aws-01') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 0 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 6 repaired <- join_repair(sensors, metadata, by = "station", standardize_case = "lower") #> ✔ Repaired 4 value(s) key_check(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "station") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

title: Common Join Problems
text: key contains non-breaking space (U+00A0) instead regular space, zero-width joiner crept copy-paste PDF. strings print identically match. "Los\u00a0Angeles" key left looks like "Los Angeles" right, non-breaking space makes different byte sequences. join_repair() remove_invisible = TRUE (default) strips : Common sources include PDF extraction, web scraping, cross-platform file transfers. join_repair() handles common offenders: non-breaking spaces, zero-width joiners, BOM markers, soft hyphens. attempt full Unicode normalization (NFC vs. NFD); reach stringi::stri_trans_nfc(). join_spy() still flags encoding issues repair, suggest_repairs() report print exact R code needed.
code: # Simulate a non-breaking space in one key left <- data.frame(   city = c("New York", "Los\u00a0Angeles", "Chicago"),   pop = c(8.3, 3.9, 2.7),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  right <- data.frame(   city = c("New York", "Los Angeles", "Chicago"),   area = c(302, 469, 227),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(left, right, by = "city") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: city #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 1 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left column 'city' has encoding issues (invisible chars or mixed encoding) #> ℹ 1 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'Los Angeles' ~ 'Los Angeles') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 2 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 4 left_fixed <- join_repair(left, by = "city") #> ✔ Repaired 1 value(s) key_check(left_fixed, right, by = "city") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

title: Common Join Problems
text: Empty strings ("") valid character values R. match empty strings join, almost never want: two rows missing identifiers get joined though refer entity. Converting empties NA joining fixes , since NAs never match R: Note data.table treats "" NA_character_ distinct keyed joins, using data.table backend need convert empty strings NA sides.
code: patients <- data.frame(   mrn = c("P001", "", "P003"),   age = c(34, 56, 29),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  visits <- data.frame(   mrn = c("P001", "P002", ""),   date = c("2024-01-10", "2024-02-15", "2024-03-20"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(patients, visits, by = "mrn") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: mrn #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 1 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ℹ Left column 'mrn' has 1 empty string(s) - these match other empty strings but not NA #> ℹ Right column 'mrn' has 1 empty string(s) - these match other empty strings but not NA #> ℹ 2 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'P003' ~ 'P001', 'P003' ~ 'P002') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: NA #> left_join: NA #> right_join: NA #> full_join: NA patients_fixed <- join_repair(patients, by = "mrn", empty_to_na = TRUE) #> ✔ Repaired 1 value(s) patients_fixed$mrn #> [1] "P001" NA     "P003"

title: Common Join Problems
text: Sometimes keys close identical. whitespace case problems – genuine mismatches join_spy() flags finds keys one table counterpart . Internally, join_spy() computes Levenshtein distances unmatched keys. two keys differ one two characters, report flags near-matches. clearer example multiple near-matches: "Johnson" vs. "Jonhson" (transposition), "Smithe" vs. "Smith" (extra character), "O'Brian" vs. "O'Brien" (vowel swap) within edit distance 2. "Williams" matches exactly. automated fix since joinspy know side correct, near-match list gives concrete starting point building lookup table.
code: orders <- data.frame(   sku = c("WDG-100", "GDG-200", "GZM-300"),   qty = c(5, 12, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  catalog <- data.frame(   sku = c("WDG-100", "GDG-200", "GZM-301"),   price = c(9.99, 14.99, 7.50),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(orders, catalog, by = "sku") employees <- data.frame(   name = c("Johnson", "Smithe", "O'Brian", "Williams"),   dept = c("Sales", "R&D", "Ops", "HR"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  payroll <- data.frame(   name = c("Jonhson", "Smith", "O'Brien", "Williams"),   salary = c(55000, 62000, 48000, 71000),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(employees, payroll, by = "name")

title: Common Join Problems
text: Duplicate keys cause row multiplication. left join key appears twice right table doubles corresponding rows left. key_duplicates() shows rows responsible: customer one address, deduplicate first. genuinely need combinations, multiplication correct – just need know happen. sides duplicates, key group produces Cartesian product: Product "" 2 rows left 3 right, join produces 2 x 3 = 6 rows key alone. check_cartesian() quantifies total expansion run join:
code: orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = c(1, 2, 3),   amount = c(100, 250, 75) )  addresses <- data.frame(   customer_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   address = c("NYC", "LA", "SF", "Chicago"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(orders, addresses, by = "customer_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: customer_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 4 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 1 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 3 #> Keys only in left: 0 #> Keys only in right: 0 #> Match rate (left): "100%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Right table has 1 duplicate key(s) affecting 2 rows - may cause row multiplication #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 4 #> left_join: 4 #> right_join: 4 #> full_join: 4 key_duplicates(addresses, by = "customer_id") #>   customer_id address .n_duplicates #> 2           2      LA             2 #> 3           2      SF             2 orders_dup <- data.frame(   product = c("A", "A", "B", "B"),   qty     = c(10, 20, 5, 15) )  prices_dup <- data.frame(   product = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B"),   price   = c(1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 2.0, 2.5) )  join_spy(orders_dup, prices_dup, by = "product") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: product #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 4 Unique keys: 2 Duplicate keys: 2 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 5 Unique keys: 2 Duplicate keys: 2 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 0 #> Keys only in right: 0 #> Match rate (left): "100%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left table has 2 duplicate key(s) affecting 4 rows - may cause row multiplication #> ! Right table has 2 duplicate key(s) affecting 5 rows - may cause row multiplication #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 10 #> left_join: 10 #> right_join: 10 #> full_join: 10 check_cartesian(orders_dup, prices_dup, by = "product") #> ✔ No Cartesian product risk (expansion factor: 2x)

title: Common Join Problems
text: NA never equals NA R. design, surprises people expect two missing values match. can either remove rows NA keys joining, replace NAs sentinel value actually want match:
code: orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = c(1, NA, 3, NA),   amount = c(100, 200, 300, 400) )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c(1, 2, 3, NA),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Unknown"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(orders, customers, by = "customer_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: customer_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 4 Unique keys: 2 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 2 #> Right table: Rows: 4 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 1 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 0 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "100%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Left table has 2 NA key(s) - these will not match #> ! Right table has 1 NA key(s) - these will not match #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 2 #> left_join: 4 #> right_join: 4 #> full_join: 6 # Remove orders_clean <- orders[!is.na(orders$customer_id), ] key_check(orders_clean, customers, by = "customer_id") #> ! Key check found 1 issue(s): #> ✖ Right table has 1 NA key(s)

title: Common Join Problems
text: One table stores IDs integers, character strings. merge() coerces silently; dplyr::left_join() refuses. Either way, want know join. subtler variant occurs Date vs. character, POSIXct vs. Date, join either fails coerces numeric intermediaries. join_spy() flags type mismatch regardless types involved.
code: invoices <- data.frame(   product_id = c(1, 2, 3),   total = c(500, 300, 150) )  products <- data.frame(   product_id = c("1", "2", "3"),   name = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(invoices, products, by = "product_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: product_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 3 #> Keys only in left: 0 #> Keys only in right: 0 #> Match rate (left): "100%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ! Type mismatch: 'product_id' is numeric, 'product_id' is character - may cause unexpected results #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 3 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 3 invoices$product_id <- as.character(invoices$product_id) key_check(invoices, products, by = "product_id") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

title: Common Join Problems
text: tables duplicate keys, get Cartesian product within key group. real data can turn 10,000-row join million-row table. detect_cardinality() tells us relationship type: expected one--many relationship, join_strict() stop us explosion happens:
code: items <- data.frame(   order_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2, 2),   item = c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  payments <- data.frame(   order_id = c(1, 1, 2, 2),   method = c("Card", "Cash", "Card", "Wire"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  check_cartesian(items, payments, by = "order_id") #> ✔ No Cartesian product risk (expansion factor: 2x) detect_cardinality(items, payments, by = "order_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "m:m" #> Left duplicates: 2 key(s) #> Right duplicates: 2 key(s) join_strict(items, payments, by = "order_id", type = "left", expect = "1:m") #> Error: #> ! Cardinality violation: expected 1:m but found m:m #>   Left duplicates: 2, Right duplicates: 2

title: Common Join Problems
text: inner join returns zero rows, downstream code may check empty data frame. Zero overlap – keys use completely different formats, amount trimming case-folding help. need mapping table transformation extracts numeric part:
code: system_a <- data.frame(   user_id = c("USR-001", "USR-002", "USR-003"),   score = c(85, 90, 78),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  system_b <- data.frame(   user_id = c("1", "2", "3"),   dept = c("Sales", "R&D", "Ops"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_spy(system_a, system_b, by = "user_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: user_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 0 #> Keys only in left: 3 #> Keys only in right: 3 #> Match rate (left): "0%" #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 0 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 6 system_a$user_num <- gsub("^USR-0*", "", system_a$user_id) key_check(system_a, system_b, by = c("user_num" = "user_id")) #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

title: Common Join Problems
text: join goes wrong, typically start join_spy(). checks string quality, key overlap, cardinality, predicted row counts one call: large datasets, passing sample = 1000 runs check random subset first. report flags whitespace, case, encoding, empty strings, join_repair() handles : predicted row count higher expected, inspect duplicates decide whether aggregate deduplicate: keys clean, join_strict() enforces cardinality expect: joined output still looks wrong, join_explain() gives breakdown happened: production pipelines, set_log_file() routes subsequent reports log file, useful debugging issues fact: pipelines multiple joins, analyze_join_chain() diagnoses entire sequence , reporting first problem enters chain.
code: report <- join_spy(x, y, by = "key_col") x_clean <- join_repair(x, by = "key_col") # or both sides: repaired <- join_repair(x, y, by = "key_col", standardize_case = "lower") key_duplicates(y, by = "key_col") result <- join_strict(x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col",                       type = "left", expect = "1:1") result <- left_join_spy(x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col") join_explain(result, x_clean, y_clean, by = "key_col") set_log_file("logs/join_diagnostics.log") # All join_spy() / join_explain() calls now append to this file

title: Getting Started with joinspy
text: joinspy provides diagnostic tools understanding debugging data frame joins R. Instead discovering join problems fact, joinspy helps identify issues upfront understand happen join. Key features: Pre-join diagnostics join_spy() key_check() Duplicate detection key_duplicates() Post-join analysis join_explain() join_diff() Safe wrappers: left_join_spy(), right_join_spy(), inner_join_spy(), full_join_spy() Cardinality enforcement join_strict() Auto-repair join_repair() suggest_repairs() Visualization plot() summary() methods

title: Getting Started with joinspy
text: joinspy analyzes join keys join, detecting: Duplicate keys (causes row multiplication) Whitespace issues (leading/trailing spaces) Case mismatches (“ABC” vs “abc”) Encoding problems (invisible Unicode characters) NA values keys Type mismatches (numeric vs character)

Cardinality violation: expected 1:1 but found 1:m #>   Left duplicates: 0, Right duplicates: 1

title: Joins in Production
text: key_check() returns single logical – TRUE issues detected, FALSE otherwise. simplest assertion wraps stopifnot(): keys problems, script halts: warn = FALSE, printed diagnostics suppressed – cron job CI pipeline, want script fail hard rather print warnings. explicit /stop gives us custom error message: can also chain assertion repair step – run key_check() first, repair failure, re-check: key_check() binary pass/fail gate; join_spy() builds full report match rates, expected row counts, categorized issues. production, key_check() runs every execution. join_spy() reach assertion fails need understand .
code: orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02", "C03", "C04"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02", "C03", "C04"),   region = c("East", "West", "East", "North"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # This passes -- keys are clean stopifnot(key_check(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) orders_dirty <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C01", "C02 ", "C03 ", "C04"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  stopifnot(key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) #> Error: #> ! key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE) is not TRUE if (!key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) {   stop("Key quality check failed for orders-customers join. ",        "Run join_spy() interactively for details.", call. = FALSE) } #> Error: #> ! Key quality check failed for orders-customers join. Run join_spy() interactively for details. ok <- key_check(orders_dirty, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)  if (!ok) {   repaired <- join_repair(     orders_dirty, customers,     by = "customer_id",     trim_whitespace = TRUE,     remove_invisible = TRUE   )   orders_clean <- repaired$x   customers_clean <- repaired$y    # Re-check after repair   stopifnot(key_check(orders_clean, customers_clean,                        by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)) } #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s)

title: Joins in Production
text: *_join_spy() wrappers print diagnostic output default. scheduled script, .quiet = TRUE suppresses printed output still computing report internally. report still available via last_report(): join runs silently; later, pull report check contents programmatically: report object plain list, standard R subsetting works arbitrarily complex validation logic. One caveat: last_report() stores recent report. script performs three joins sequence, third report survives. retain earlier reports, capture explicitly:
code: sensors <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("S01", "S02", "S03", "S04"),   location = c("Roof", "Basement", "Lobby", "Garage"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  readings <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("S01", "S02", "S03", "S05"),   temperature = c(22.1, 18.5, 21.0, 19.3),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Nothing printed result <- left_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) rpt <- last_report() rpt$match_analysis$match_rate #> [1] 0.75 rpt <- last_report() if (rpt$match_analysis$match_rate < 0.95) {   warning(sprintf(     "Low match rate (%.1f%%) in sensor join -- check for missing sensor IDs",     rpt$match_analysis$match_rate * 100   )) } #> Warning: Low match rate (75.0%) in sensor join -- check for missing sensor IDs result1 <- left_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) report1 <- last_report()  # ... later ... result2 <- inner_join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", .quiet = TRUE) report2 <- last_report()

title: Joins in Production
text: join one--one development can become many--many production upstream data changes. join_strict() enforces cardinality constraint throws error violated. development, use detect_cardinality() understand actual relationship: One--many: product appears products can appear multiple times line_items. encode expectation production: someone loads products table duplicate product IDs, script fails immediately: four cardinality levels: 1:1 – lookup lookup. key appears exactly sides. 1:m – reference left, transactions right (products line items, stations hourly readings). m:1 – transactions left, lookup right (sales joined region table). m:m – duplicates sides. Almost always bug; requiring explicit expect = "m:m" acts speed bump. practice, "1:m" "m:1" cover production joins. detect_cardinality() confirms relationship development; expect value hard-coded production script. check_cartesian() solves different problem: warns Cartesian product explosion key many duplicates sides. join can violate "1:1" constraint without triggering Cartesian explosion (one extra duplicate enough), "m:m" join can produce massive product join_strict() allow. two functions complement .
code: products <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P2", "P3"),   name = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  line_items <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3", "P3"),   order_id = c(101, 102, 103, 104, 105),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  detect_cardinality(products, line_items, by = "product_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "1:m" #> Right duplicates: 2 key(s) result <- join_strict(   products, line_items,   by = "product_id",   type = "left",   expect = "1:m" ) nrow(result) #> [1] 5 products_bad <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3"),   name = c("Widget", "Widget v2", "Gadget", "Gizmo"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_strict(   products_bad, line_items,   by = "product_id",   type = "left",   expect = "1:m" ) #> Error: #> ! Cardinality violation: expected 1:m but found m:m #>   Left duplicates: 1, Right duplicates: 2

text: log_report() writes single report file. format depends file extension: Text format works tailing logs batch run; JSON format feeds monitoring systems downstream scripts. Reports can also saved .rds files, preserves full R object later interactive inspection.
code: report <- join_spy(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id")  # Text format -- human-readable txt_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".log") log_report(report, txt_log) #> ✔ Report logged to C:/Temp\Rtmp2BnYNR\file31bf43ca12605.log cat(readLines(txt_log), sep = "\n") #> Logged: 2026-03-31 23:21:09 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: sensor_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 4 #>   Unique keys: 4 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 3 #>   Keys only in left: 1 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 75% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 3 #>   left_join: 4 #>   right_join: 4 #>   full_join: 5 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   near_match: 1 #> ============================================================ unlink(txt_log) # JSON format -- machine-readable json_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".json") log_report(report, json_log) #> ✔ Report logged to C:/Temp\Rtmp2BnYNR\file31bf41787369d.json cat(readLines(json_log), sep = "\n") #> { #>   "by": "sensor_id", #>   "x_summary": { #>   "n_rows": 4, #>   "n_unique": 4, #>   "n_duplicated": null, #>   "n_na": 0 #> }, #>   "y_summary": { #>   "n_rows": 4, #>   "n_unique": 4, #>   "n_duplicated": null, #>   "n_na": 0 #> }, #>   "match_analysis": { #>   "n_matched": 3, #>   "n_left_only": 1, #>   "n_right_only": 1, #>   "match_rate": 0.75 #> }, #>   "expected_rows": { #>   "inner": 3, #>   "left": 4, #>   "right": 4, #>   "full": 5 #> }, #>   "n_issues": 1, #>   "issue_types": "near_match", #>   "logged_at": "2026-03-31 23:21:09" #> } unlink(json_log)

title: Joins in Production
text: sample parameter join_spy() runs analysis random subset actual join (via *_join_spy() wrapper) still operates full data. sampled report approximate – match rates duplicate counts estimated subset. production monitoring, typically care whether match rate roughly 95% roughly 60%, whether 94.7% 95.1%. Sampling catches systemic problems (wrong key column, widespread encoding issues, duplicate explosion) fraction runtime. Sampling can miss rare issues. 0.1% keys zero-width space, 5,000-row sample 10-million-row table might include . Running full diagnostics periodically (weekly, upstream source changes) alongside sampled daily runs covers speed thoroughness.
code: # Simulate a large dataset set.seed(42) big_orders <- data.frame(   customer_id = sample(paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:5000)), 50000, replace = TRUE),   amount = round(runif(50000, 10, 500), 2),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  big_customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = paste0("C", sprintf("%04d", 1:6000)),   region = sample(c("North", "South", "East", "West"), 6000, replace = TRUE),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Full analysis system.time(report_full <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers, by = "customer_id")) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.11    0.06    0.18  # Sampled analysis system.time(report_sampled <- join_spy(big_orders, big_customers,                                         by = "customer_id", sample = 5000)) #>    user  system elapsed  #>    0.09    0.00    0.09

title: Joins in Production
text: realistic production workflow: nightly job loads order customer data, validates keys, repairs needed, joins cardinality enforcement, logs everything. three gates catch different failure modes: key_check() catches string-level problems attempts repair, detect_cardinality() halts unexpected many--many relationships, row count check guards anything first two gates missed. Logging runs throughout set_log_file() called top. structure scales – tables, gates, joins, pattern.
code: # ============================================================ # Nightly order enrichment pipeline # ============================================================  # --- Setup logging --- pipeline_log <- tempfile(fileext = ".log") set_log_file(pipeline_log, format = "text") #> ℹ Automatic logging enabled: C:/Temp\Rtmp2BnYNR\file31bf4120083b.log  # --- Load data (simulated) --- orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:6,   customer_id = c("C001", "C002 ", "C003", "C003", "C004", "C005"),   amount = c(150, 230, 89, 410, 320, 175),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("C001", "C002", "C003", "C004", "C005", "C006"),   name = c("Acme Corp", "Globex", "Initech", "Umbrella", "Soylent", "Wonka"),   tier = c("gold", "silver", "gold", "bronze", "silver", "gold"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # --- Gate 1: key quality assertion --- keys_ok <- key_check(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", warn = FALSE)  if (!keys_ok) {   message("Key issues detected -- attempting repair")   repaired <- join_repair(     orders, customers,     by = "customer_id",     trim_whitespace = TRUE,     remove_invisible = TRUE   )   orders <- repaired$x   customers <- repaired$y } #> Key issues detected -- attempting repair #> ✔ Repaired 1 value(s)  # --- Gate 2: cardinality check --- card <- detect_cardinality(orders, customers, by = "customer_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "m:1" #> Left duplicates: 1 key(s) if (card == "m:m") {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Unexpected m:m cardinality in orders-customers join", call. = FALSE) }  # --- Join (with auto-logging via *_join_spy) --- enriched <- left_join_spy(orders, customers, by = "customer_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # --- Gate 3: row count sanity check --- # A left join should never lose rows from the left table if (nrow(enriched) < nrow(orders)) {   set_log_file(NULL)   unlink(pipeline_log)   stop("Row count decreased after left join -- possible data corruption",        call. = FALSE) }  # --- Output --- message(sprintf("Pipeline complete: %d enriched orders", nrow(enriched))) #> Pipeline complete: 6 enriched orders head(enriched) #>   customer_id order_id amount      name   tier #> 1        C001        1    150 Acme Corp   gold #> 2        C002        2    230    Globex silver #> 3        C003        3     89   Initech   gold #> 4        C003        4    410   Initech   gold #> 5        C004        5    320  Umbrella bronze #> 6        C005        6    175   Soylent silver  # --- Review the log --- if (file.exists(pipeline_log)) {   cat(readLines(pipeline_log), sep = "\n") } #>  #> Logged: 2026-03-31 23:21:10 #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #> Join Key: customer_id #>  #> Left Table (x): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 5 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Right Table (y): #>   Rows: 6 #>   Unique keys: 6 #>   Duplicated keys:  #>   NA keys: 0 #>  #> Match Analysis: #>   Keys in both: 5 #>   Keys only in left: 0 #>   Keys only in right: 1 #>   Match rate: 100% #>  #> Expected Rows: #>   inner_join: 6 #>   left_join: 6 #>   right_join: 7 #>   full_join: 7 #>  #> Issues Detected: 1 #>   duplicates: 1 #> ============================================================  # --- Cleanup --- set_log_file(NULL) #> ℹ Automatic logging disabled unlink(pipeline_log)

title: Quick Start
text: Trailing leading whitespace common reason keys fail match. column exported Excel might contain "London " alongside "London", R treats distinct values. report flags whitespace problems shows values affected. "Paris " " Berlin" fail match, dropping match rate 100% 50%. Whitespace problems compound multi-column keys: London fails district column, Paris city column. row needs key columns match, single trailing space column breaks join. quick pass/fail gate, can use key_check(): key_check() returns FALSE whitespace, warn = TRUE (default) prints issues found. returns single logical, slots stopifnot() conditional logic.
code: sales <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris ", " Berlin", "Tokyo"),   revenue = c(500, 300, 450, 600),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  cities <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris", "Berlin", "Tokyo", "Madrid"),   country = c("UK", "France", "Germany", "Japan", "Spain"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(sales, cities, by = "city") sales2 <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris ", "Berlin"),   district = c("West ", "Central", " Mitte"),   revenue = c(500, 300, 450),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  districts <- data.frame(   city = c("London", "Paris", "Berlin"),   district = c("West", "Central", "Mitte"),   pop = c(200000, 350000, 180000),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(sales2, districts, by = c("city", "district")) key_check(sales, cities, by = "city") #> ! Key check found 1 issue(s): #> ✖ Left table column 'city' has whitespace issues (2 values)

title: Quick Start
text: CRM might store "ACME" billing system stores "Acme", R treats distinct strings. "Initech" matches exactly; three lost standard join. match_analysis section shows potential match rate without case sensitivity.
code: invoices <- data.frame(   company = c("ACME", "globex", "Initech", "UMBRELLA"),   amount = c(1200, 800, 950, 1500),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  vendors <- data.frame(   company = c("Acme", "Globex", "Initech", "Umbrella"),   sector = c("Manufacturing", "Logistics", "Software", "Biotech"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(invoices, vendors, by = "company")

title: Quick Start
text: Two strings can render identically console differ byte level: non-breaking space (\u00A0) versus regular space, zero-width space PDF copy-paste. zero-width space appended "SKU-003" invisible printed prevents match. joinspy flags encoding issues identifies specific Unicode code points involved.
code: # Simulate invisible character contamination raw_ids <- data.frame(   product_id = c("SKU-001", "SKU-002", paste0("SKU-003", "\u200B")),   batch = c("A", "B", "C"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  clean_ids <- data.frame(   product_id = c("SKU-001", "SKU-002", "SKU-003"),   warehouse = c("East", "West", "North"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(raw_ids, clean_ids, by = "product_id")

title: Quick Start
text: Real data rarely just one problem. join_spy() checks whitespace, case, encoding issues one call reports together, grouped type. Zero matches four rows, problems grouped type. fix differs per category: whitespace safe trim automatically, case standardization requires choosing direction, encoding issues may indicate deeper pipeline problems.
code: employees <- data.frame(   dept = c("Sales ", "ENGINEERING", "marketing", paste0("HR", "\u00A0")),   name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  departments <- data.frame(   dept = c("Sales", "Engineering", "Marketing", "HR"),   budget = c(50000, 80000, 45000, 30000),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(employees, departments, by = "dept")

title: Quick Start
text: Duplicates don’t prevent matches, multiply rows. "London" appears three times left table twice right, get six rows London inner join. key_duplicates() returns offending rows along count: keep argument accepts "first", "last", default "". .n_duplicates column maps directly row multiplication factor join.
code: transactions <- data.frame(   store_id = c("S1", "S1", "S2", "S3", "S3", "S3"),   day = c("Mon", "Tue", "Mon", "Mon", "Tue", "Wed"),   amount = c(100, 200, 150, 300, 250, 400),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  key_duplicates(transactions, by = "store_id") #>   store_id day amount .n_duplicates #> 1       S1 Mon    100             2 #> 2       S1 Tue    200             2 #> 4       S3 Mon    300             3 #> 5       S3 Tue    250             3 #> 6       S3 Wed    400             3

title: Quick Start
text: join_repair() handles whitespace trimming, case standardization, invisible character removal, empty--NA conversion single call.

title: Quick Start
text: can preview join_repair() change dry_run = TRUE: dry run shows many values change become, without touching original data.
code: messy_left <- data.frame(   id = c(" A-101", "B-202 ", "c-303", paste0("D-404", "\u200B")),   score = c(88, 92, 76, 95),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  messy_right <- data.frame(   id = c("A-101", "B-202", "C-303", "D-404"),   label = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_repair(messy_left, messy_right, by = "id", dry_run = TRUE) #>  #> ── Repair Preview (Dry Run) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Left table (x) ── #>  #> ℹ id: trimmed whitespace (2), removed invisible chars (1)

title: Quick Start
text: Drop dry_run (set FALSE) get back repaired data frames. pass x y, return value list : sides now clean, uppercase, whitespace-free keys. can confirm key_check(): join_repair() fixes mechanical string problems: whitespace, case, invisible characters, empty strings. fix typos, semantic mismatches, different key formats ("USA" vs. "United States").
code: repaired <- join_repair(   messy_left, messy_right,   by = "id",   trim_whitespace = TRUE,   standardize_case = "upper",   remove_invisible = TRUE ) #> ✔ Repaired 4 value(s)  repaired$x$id #> [1] "A-101" "B-202" "C-303" "D-404" repaired$y$id #> [1] "A-101" "B-202" "C-303" "D-404" key_check(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "id") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected

code: report <- join_spy(messy_left, messy_right, by = "id") suggest_repairs(report) #>  #> ── Suggested Repairs ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> x$id <- trimws(x$id) #> # Remove invisible characters: #> x$id <- gsub("[\u200B\u200C\u200D\uFEFF\u00A0]", "", x$id, perl = TRUE) #> # Standardize case: #> x$id <- tolower(x$id) #> y$id <- tolower(y$id)

title: Quick Start
text: Every JoinReport includes expected_rows component predicts many rows join type produce, calculated key overlap duplicate structure. left join retains five order rows (NA unmatched "P4"), inner join drops . join type, calculation counts overlapping keys multiplies number duplicates side. key appearing twice x three times y contributes 2 * 3 = 6 rows inner join. calculation exact keys non-NA string issues remain. NA keys deserve caution: R’s merge() dplyr skip NAs default, predicted count may overestimate. report warns NAs present. summary() returns information compact data frame:
code: orders <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P1", "P2", "P3", "P4"),   quantity = c(10, 5, 20, 15, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  products <- data.frame(   product_id = c("P1", "P2", "P3", "P5"),   name = c("Widget", "Gadget", "Gizmo", "Doohickey"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  report <- join_spy(orders, products, by = "product_id") report$expected_rows #> $inner #> [1] 4 #>  #> $left #> [1] 5 #>  #> $right #> [1] 5 #>  #> $full #> [1] 6 summary(report) #>               metric value #> 1          left_rows  5.00 #> 2         right_rows  4.00 #> 3   left_unique_keys  4.00 #> 4  right_unique_keys  4.00 #> 5       keys_matched  3.00 #> 6     keys_left_only  1.00 #> 7    keys_right_only  1.00 #> 8         match_rate  0.75 #> 9             issues  1.00 #> 10   inner_join_rows  4.00 #> 11    left_join_rows  5.00 #> 12   right_join_rows  5.00 #> 13    full_join_rows  6.00

title: Quick Start
text: join_explain() takes result, two input tables, join column, explains row count changed: output decomposes row count contributions matched keys, unmatched keys, duplicate-driven expansion. Usually table grows unexpectedly, answer handful keys duplicates one side.
code: tickets <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   seat = c("A1", "B2", "B3", "C1"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  events <- data.frame(   event_id = c(1, 2, 4),   name = c("Concert", "Play", "Opera"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- merge(tickets, events, by = "event_id") join_explain(result, tickets, events, by = "event_id", type = "inner") #>  #> ── Join Explanation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Row Counts ── #>  #> Left table (x): 4 rows #> Right table (y): 3 rows #> Result: 3 rows #> ℹ Result has 1 fewer rows than left table #>  #> ── Why the row count changed ── #>  #> ℹ Left table has 1 duplicate key(s) - each match creates multiple rows #> ℹ Inner join dropped 1 unmatched left rows

title: Quick Start
text: join_diff() compares data frame join show rows added, dropped, preserved: Row 1 dropped (match right table non-full join irrelevant since used = TRUE; row 5 added right table). second example highlights row addition duplicates rather new keys: row count grew three four id = 1 matched two rows lookup. new keys introduced; growth came entirely duplicate expansion. join_diff() distinguishes two sources row addition (new keys vs. duplicate expansion).
code: before <- data.frame(   id = 1:4,   value = c("a", "b", "c", "d"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(2, 3, 4, 5),   extra = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after <- merge(before, lookup, by = "id", all = TRUE) join_diff(before, after, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 4 rows x 2 columns #> After: 5 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: extra #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 4 #> Unique keys after: 5 before2 <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 2, 3),   value = c("a", "b", "c"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  dup_lookup <- data.frame(   id = c(1, 1, 2, 3),   tag = c("X", "Y", "Z", "W"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  after2 <- merge(before2, dup_lookup, by = "id") join_diff(before2, after2, by = "id") #>  #> ── Join Diff ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Dimensions ── #>  #> Before: 3 rows x 2 columns #> After: 4 rows x 3 columns #> Change: "+1" rows, "+1" columns #>  #> ── Column Changes ── #>  #> ✔ Added: tag #>  #> ── Key Analysis ── #>  #> Unique keys before: 3 #> Unique keys after: 3 #> Duplicate rows: "+2"

title: Quick Start
text: *_join_spy() family wraps standard joins automatic diagnostics. print report joining return joined data frame: four wrappers (left_join_spy, right_join_spy, inner_join_spy, full_join_spy) share interface. wrappers convenient exploratory work; production, might prefer calling join_spy() first inspecting report programmatically proceeding. wrappers also accept backend parameter ("base", "dplyr", "data.table") overrides automatic backend detection.
code: patients <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P03"),   age = c(34, 28, 45),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  labs <- data.frame(   patient_id = c("P01", "P02", "P04"),   result = c(5.2, 3.8, 6.1),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  result <- left_join_spy(patients, labs, by = "patient_id") #>  #> ── Join Diagnostic Report ────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Join columns: patient_id #>  #> ── Table Summary ── #>  #> Left table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #> Right table: Rows: 3 Unique keys: 3 Duplicate keys: 0 NA keys: 0 #>  #> ── Match Analysis ── #>  #> Keys in both: 2 #> Keys only in left: 1 #> Keys only in right: 1 #> Match rate (left): "66.7%" #>  #> ── Issues Detected ── #>  #> ℹ 3 near-match(es) found (e.g., 'P03' ~ 'P01', 'P03' ~ 'P02', 'P03' ~ 'P04') - possible typos? #>  #> ── Expected Row Counts ── #>  #> inner_join: 2 #> left_join: 3 #> right_join: 3 #> full_join: 4 #> ℹ Performing "left" join... #> ✔ Result: 3 rows (as expected) head(result) #>   patient_id age result #> 1        P01  34    5.2 #> 2        P02  28    3.8 #> 3        P03  45     NA

title: Quick Start
text: pipelines, printed output can distracting. .quiet = TRUE, report suppressed can retrieved afterward via last_report(): last_report() returns report recent *_join_spy() call persists duration R session.
code: result <- inner_join_spy(patients, labs, by = "patient_id", .quiet = TRUE)  # Later, inspect what happened rpt <- last_report() rpt$match_analysis$match_rate #> [1] 0.6666667

title: Quick Start
text: join_strict() performs join errors key relationship violates stated expectation. guard unexpected many--many joins multiply rows. expect argument accepts four cardinality levels: "1:1": sides unique keys. duplicate either side error. "1:m": left table unique keys, right may repeat. classic lookup-table join. "m:1": mirror "1:m". right table lookup. "m:m": duplicates allowed sides. Rarely want; exists explicit opt-intentional Cartesian products per key. detect actual cardinality without enforcing anything: returns string like "1:m" can use programmatically, e.g. select right expect value log cardinality monitoring.
code: sensors <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T2", "T3"),   location = c("Roof", "Basement", "Lobby"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  readings <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T2", "T3"),   value = c(22.1, 18.5, 21.0),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Succeeds: one reading per sensor join_strict(sensors, readings, by = "sensor_id", expect = "1:1") #>   sensor_id location value #> 1        T1     Roof  22.1 #> 2        T2 Basement  18.5 #> 3        T3    Lobby  21.0 # Fails: T1 has two readings, violating 1:1 readings_dup <- data.frame(   sensor_id = c("T1", "T1", "T2", "T3"),   value = c(22.1, 23.4, 18.5, 21.0),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  join_strict(sensors, readings_dup, by = "sensor_id", expect = "1:1") #> Error: #> ! Cardinality violation: expected 1:1 but found 1:m #>   Left duplicates: 0, Right duplicates: 1 detect_cardinality(sensors, readings_dup, by = "sensor_id") #> ℹ Detected cardinality: "1:m" #> Right duplicates: 1 key(s)

title: Quick Start
text: tables duplicates key, join produces Cartesian product duplicates. Two keys 100 rows produce 10,000 rows key alone. check_cartesian() warns happens: threshold argument (default 10) controls large expansion factor must warning fires.
code: left <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "A", "A", "B"),   x = 1:4,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  right <- data.frame(   group = c("A", "A", "B", "B"),   y = 5:8,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  check_cartesian(left, right, by = "group") #> ✔ No Cartesian product risk (expansion factor: 2x)

title: Quick Start
text: analyze_join_chain() runs diagnostics across sequence joins. provide named list tables list join specifications: step prints diagnostic report. special name "result" refers output previous join, can chain many tables needed. "result" resolves actual intermediate data, key diagnostics step two account NAs key distribution changes introduced first join.
code: orders <- data.frame(   order_id = 1:4,   customer_id = c(1, 2, 2, 3),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  customers <- data.frame(   customer_id = 1:3,   region_id = c(1, 1, 2),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  regions <- data.frame(   region_id = 1:2,   name = c("North", "South"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  analyze_join_chain(   tables = list(orders = orders, customers = customers, regions = regions),   joins = list(     list(left = "orders", right = "customers", by = "customer_id"),     list(left = "result", right = "regions", by = "region_id")   ) ) #>  #> ── Join Chain Analysis ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Step 1: orders + customers ── #>  #> Left: 4 rows #> Right: 3 rows #> Match rate: 100% #> Expected result: 4 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Step 2: result + regions ── #>  #> Left: 4 rows #> Right: 2 rows #> Match rate: 100% #> Expected result: 4 rows (left join) #> ! 1 issue(s) detected #>  #> ── Chain Summary ── #>  #> ! Total issues across chain: 2

code: # Force dplyr backend even for plain data frames left_join_spy(x, y, by = "id", backend = "dplyr")  # Force base R for tibbles left_join_spy(x, y, by = "id", backend = "base")

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: retail analytics team receives monthly sales data distribution partner CSV exported Excel. join internal customer database customer_id. two quarters, everything works. one month, 30% sales records stop matching. Nobody changed code customer database. partner’s IDs present internal system – appears. Nothing str() print() reveals issue – trailing spaces invisible console output. join_spy() catches : Three six partner IDs carry whitespace. "CUST-1002 " different string "CUST-1002" far R concerned. join_repair() trims tables : can verify repair worked: now join gives us expected: root cause Excel CONCATENATE formula preserved trailing spaces variable-width source column. Excel renders "CUST-1002" "CUST-1002 " identically, nobody noticed. Trailing whitespace single common join failure see practice.
code: partner_sales <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("CUST-1001", "CUST-1002 ", "CUST-1003",                   " CUST-1004", "CUST-1005 ", "CUST-1006"),   amount = c(2500, 1800, 3200, 950, 4100, 1600),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  internal_db <- data.frame(   customer_id = c("CUST-1001", "CUST-1002", "CUST-1003",                   "CUST-1004", "CUST-1005", "CUST-1006", "CUST-1007"),   region = c("West", "East", "West", "South", "East", "North", "West"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(partner_sales, internal_db, by = "customer_id") repaired <- join_repair(partner_sales, internal_db, by = "customer_id") #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) partner_fixed <- repaired$x internal_fixed <- repaired$y key_check(partner_fixed, internal_fixed, by = "customer_id") #> ✔ Key check passed: no issues detected result <- merge(partner_fixed, internal_fixed, by = "customer_id") nrow(result) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: SaaS company wants join CRM profiles clickstream events churn analysis. CRM stores email addresses uppercase (database migration decision late 1990s). web app stores lowercase. systems internally consistent. inner join returns zero rows. R’s string comparison case-sensitive, every key pair fails. join_spy() flags situation join: suggest_repairs() generates fix: can use join_repair() directly, specifying case standardization: lowercasing sides, inner join returns four matched rows (everyone except Eve, click data, Frank, CRM): Email addresses case-insensitive RFC 5321, lowercasing right normalization . identifier types (product codes, country abbreviations), "upper" may appropriate.
code: crm_profiles <- data.frame(   email = c("ALICE@ACME.COM", "BOB@ACME.COM", "CAROL@ACME.COM",             "DAVE@ACME.COM", "EVE@ACME.COM"),   plan = c("enterprise", "starter", "pro", "enterprise", "starter"),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  click_events <- data.frame(   email = c("alice@acme.com", "bob@acme.com", "carol@acme.com",             "dave@acme.com", "frank@acme.com"),   page_views = c(47, 12, 89, 33, 5),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(crm_profiles, click_events, by = "email") suggest_repairs(report) #>  #> ── Suggested Repairs ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> # Standardize case: #> x$email <- tolower(x$email) #> y$email <- tolower(y$email) repaired <- join_repair(   crm_profiles, click_events,   by = "email",   standardize_case = "lower" ) #> ✔ Repaired 5 value(s) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "email") nrow(result) #> [1] 4 result #>            email       plan page_views #> 1 alice@acme.com enterprise         47 #> 2   bob@acme.com    starter         12 #> 3 carol@acme.com        pro         89 #> 4  dave@acme.com enterprise         33

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: public health researcher compiles data multiple sources systematic review. studies published supplementary tables PDF, copies table PDF viewer, pastes spreadsheet, cleans columns, reads CSV R. data looks perfect – every country name spelled correctly. half countries fail match reference population table. Printing PDF data shows nothing wrong: zero-width space “India” “France” occupies zero pixels. non-breaking space “Japan” renders like regular space U+00A0, U+0020 – trimws() always remove . merge reflects : Three six countries match. join_spy() detects invisible characters: Six matches. PDF copy-paste common source artifacts, web scraping, OCR output, legacy mainframe exports can produce . One useful debugging trick outside joinspy: nchar("India\u200B") returns 6, 5. requires already suspecting problem.
code: # Simulating PDF copy-paste artifacts: # \u00A0 is non-breaking space, \u200B is zero-width space pdf_data <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India\u200B", "Germany",               "Japan\u00A0", "Canada", "France\u200B"),   prevalence = c(12.3, 8.7, 5.1, 3.9, 6.2, 4.8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  reference <- data.frame(   country = c("Brazil", "India", "Germany", "Japan",               "Canada", "France", "Italy"),   population_m = c(214, 1408, 84, 125, 38, 68, 59),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) pdf_data$country #> [1] "Brazil"  "India​"   "Germany" "Japan "  "Canada"  "France​" nrow(merge(pdf_data, reference, by = "country")) #> [1] 3 report <- join_spy(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") repaired <- join_repair(pdf_data, reference, by = "country") #> ✔ Repaired 3 value(s) nrow(merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "country")) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: e-commerce pipeline joins transaction records product catalogue. pipeline ran cleanly months, match rates started drifting: 99% January, 97% February, 94% March. Nobody noticed finance flagged margin discrepancy April. code changed. new data entry clerk joined warehouse team December. canonical product code format "WDG-100" – uppercase prefix, dash, three-digit suffix. new clerk sometimes omitted dash, sometimes typed lowercase. warehouse system fuzzy matching internally, accepted codes. ETL join . codes match , makes partial failures harder spot complete ones. scenario differs previous ones. join_repair() can fix case issue, insert missing dashes – requires domain knowledge code format. can dry run see join_repair() fix: applying mechanical fixes: case issues resolved, missing dashes remain. manual transformation handles : eight transactions match. join_repair() handles context-free transformations (trimming, case normalization, stripping invisible characters). Inserting dash "WDG102" requires knowing canonical format – fix come someone understands data.
code: # Product catalogue (canonical format) catalogue <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG-102",                    "WDG-103", "WDG-104", "WDG-105"),   product_name = c("Widget A", "Widget B", "Widget C",                    "Widget D", "Widget E", "Widget F"),   margin = c(0.35, 0.28, 0.42, 0.31, 0.39, 0.25),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Recent transactions (mix of old and new clerk entries) transactions <- data.frame(   product_code = c("WDG-100", "WDG-101", "WDG102",                    "wdg-103", "WDG-104", "wdg105",                    "WDG-100", "WDG103"),   quantity = c(5, 3, 7, 2, 4, 6, 1, 8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) report <- join_spy(transactions, catalogue, by = "product_code") join_repair(transactions, catalogue,             by = "product_code",             standardize_case = "upper",             dry_run = TRUE) #>  #> ── Repair Preview (Dry Run) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────── #>  #> ── Left table (x) ── #>  #> ℹ product_code: upper case (2) repaired <- join_repair(transactions, catalogue,                         by = "product_code",                         standardize_case = "upper") #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) # Manual fix: insert dash if missing in product codes matching the pattern fix_codes <- function(codes) {   gsub("^([A-Z]{3})(\\d)", "\\1-\\2", codes) } repaired$x$product_code <- fix_codes(repaired$x$product_code) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = "product_code") nrow(result) #> [1] 8

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: Two government datasets need linked: regional economic indicators regional population estimates, keyed region year. year column numeric matches without trouble. region column whitespace problem affects records. compound key join, columns must match. year column fine everywhere, "Asia Pacific " trailing space match "Asia Pacific". Four rows instead six. North America Europe match; Asia Pacific , trailing space. join_spy() pinpoints column compound key problem: Six rows. compound keys, string issue single column enough break match. columns key, places byte-level discrepancy can occur.
code: economics <- data.frame(   region = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific ",              "North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific "),   year = c(2022, 2022, 2022, 2023, 2023, 2023),   gdp_growth = c(2.1, 1.8, 4.2, 1.9, 0.9, 3.8),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  population <- data.frame(   region = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific",              "North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"),   year = c(2022, 2022, 2022, 2023, 2023, 2023),   pop_millions = c(580, 450, 4300, 585, 448, 4350),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) merged <- merge(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) nrow(merged) #> [1] 4 report <- join_spy(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) repaired <- join_repair(economics, population, by = c("region", "year")) #> ✔ Repaired 2 value(s) result <- merge(repaired$x, repaired$y, by = c("region", "year")) nrow(result) #> [1] 6

title: Why Your Keys Don't Match
text: five scenarios share three properties. data looks correct standard inspection tools – str(), summary(), print() render values identically. R returns fewer () rows without warning, key values genuinely differ byte level. fix mechanical cause known – trimming, lowercasing, stripping invisible Unicode one-line operations. join_spy() surfaces cause directly, especially useful data external sources, manual entry, PDF extraction, cross-system integrations.

title: Strict Join with Cardinality Enforcement — join_strict
text: x data frame (left table). y data frame (right table). character vector column names join . type Character. type join perform. One "left" (default), "right", "inner", "full". expect Character. expected cardinality relationship. One : "1:1" key x matches one key y, vice versa "1:m" "1:many" key x can match multiple keys y, key y matches one key x "m:1" "many:1" key y can match multiple keys x, key x matches one key y "m:m" "many:many" cardinality constraints (allows relationships) backend Character NULL. join backend use. NULL (default), auto-detects input class. See left_join_spy() details. ... Additional arguments passed underlying join function.

title: Strict Join with Cardinality Enforcement — join_strict
text: joined data frame cardinality constraint satisfied. Throws error constraint violated.

title: Strict Join with Cardinality Enforcement — join_strict
code: orders <- data.frame(id = 1:3, product = c("A", "B", "C")) customers <- data.frame(id = 1:3, name = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol"))  # This succeeds (1:1 relationship) join_strict(orders, customers, by = "id", expect = "1:1")  # This fails if customers had duplicate ids (wrapped in try to show error) customers_dup <- data.frame(id = c(1, 1, 2), name = c("A1", "A2", "B")) try(join_strict(orders, customers_dup, by = "id", expect = "1:1"))
